posts.text: Every winter I swear I’m going to move. Then every summer in New Hampshire, I can’t imagine living anywhere else. I’m taking a break from Saturday website editing to relax and appreciate where I live, and to be grateful for the season of life I’m in. There were years when it felt like success meant more, faster, bigger. Now, success looks like meaningful work, trusted partnerships, a business built on referrals and long-term relationships, and the ability to be home when it matters. I’m deeply aware that this combination is a privilege, and I don’t take a single summer day like this for granted. Winter will come, and I’ll inevitably daydream about warmer zip codes! But today, in this moment, I’m exactly where I’m supposed to be, enjoying my iced espresso on our front porch, on a near perfect NH summer day.
posts.hook: Every winter I swear I’m going to move.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Solopreneur life isn't for everyone. And that's okay. There's a version of this story that gets sold a lot. Leave the corporate world, do your thing, never look back. Do what you want, make a lot of money, go where you want, work where and when you want. That may be true for some people, but it's definitely not true for everyone. Solopreneur life asks a lot of you. You are the strategy and the admin. The salesperson and the bookkeeper. The big vision and the broken printer. There's no team to absorb your vacation time, no salary that shows up regardless of how the month went, no one else to make the call when you're not sure what the right call is. (Yes, there's an AI for that, and you can hire people, but that isn't where most people start.) For some people, like me, this life is energizing. And this continues to surprise me. The autonomy is the whole point. I get to build something that looks exactly the way I want it to look, I get to work with people I actually want to work with, and design my days around what matters to me. For others, it's a grind that wears them down. They got into this life expecting something, and instead had less time with clients than they thought. If they have clients. I'm seeing more and more people going back into the corporate world after spending some time on their own. And there's nothing wrong with that. We do people a disservice when we sell the solopreneur path as universally better. It isn't better. It's different. I got into this work because I was really focused on the work I wanted to do, and building a life after grief and loss, where I got to spend more time with the people I love. That all helps me deal with the uncertainty (because that's the hardest part for me), rejection and all of the business things I don't want to deal with but have to. I don't think I've shared enough of the hard stuff here, though I do when I talk with people. My business is growing, but there is also the ever present worry of: What if it all ends tomorrow? Both things can be true. And I knew that going into this life that I love. If you are thinking of quitting corporate, ask yourself: Do you want to run a business, or do you want to do the work the business is built around? Those are two different questions. Plenty of brilliant people love the craft and would be miserable carrying everything else that comes with it. There is absolutely no shame in that. If this life is for you, you already feel it. If it isn't, the best thing you can do is be honest about that too. For many, the goal is not to be a solopreneur. The goal is to build a life that fits. Sometimes those are the same thing. Sometimes they aren't. Inspired by a conversation with T. Tara Turk-Haynes this week, and a post by Madison Butler 🏳️🌈🦄, CPT.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: I'm back on a panel with Hacking HR, this time moderating "Redesigning Work for AI at Scale" on Thursday, June 18 at 10:45AM ET. I'll be joined by some incredible panelists! Kayshia Kruger, MSOD, Anuj Kathuria, Hernan Chiosso, CSPO, SPHR 💡, Lisseth Zouhbi and Marina Krstic, I look forward to learning from you! This is a free, fully virtual summit that will bring together thousands of leaders and practitioners to explore how AI is transforming the way we work, lead, and build organizations. I hope to see you there! Register for free: https://theaixsummit.com
posts.hook: I'm back on a panel with Hacking HR, this time moderating "Redesigning Work for AI at Scale" on Thursday, June 18 at 10:45AM ET.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: I've been sitting with this one for a few weeks, since it took me awhile to process. Guusto named me to their list of HR and people experts worth following, in the Frontline & Workforce Strategy category. Any kind of recognition is great, of course. But this kind of recognition, for the work I do in the Frontline space, means so much more. Roughly 80% of the global workforce are frontline, deskless workers. The folks in warehouses, on manufacturing floors, behind the counter. But most HR strategy, most workplace tech, most of the conversation, is still built for the 20% at desks. That 80% is my entire focus. These are the companies and people I worked with everyday for nearly my entire 30 year career leading HR, and it's the work I do now for distribution and manufacturing leaders every day. So to be recognized in the frontline category specifically? I'm incredibly grateful. Thank you, Guusto! Check out the full list. Some of my friends who are doing great work are on here, and many others I follow and admire! https://lnkd.in/dxR5RHF3
posts.hook: I've been sitting with this one for a few weeks, since it took me awhile to process.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: In the past year, I've spoken with distribution and manufacturing companies with 50% turnover. 98%. 120%. 300%. What they usually say... "That's just the industry." What I usually say.... "It's not the industry. It's you." (In a kinder, gentler way!) Because behind every one of those numbers is a person. The forklift operator who trained three replacements before quitting herself. The supervisor promoted with zero support, underperforming because he didn't know the expectations of the role. The good people who gave you everything and left anyway because no one ever asked them to stay. I won't work with companies that aren't willing to look in the mirror and admit something's wrong. The ones I do work with already know there's an issue. They're just not sure what it is, or what it's costing them. I wrote about it in last week's newsletter. And I built a turnover calculator that shows companies what walking out the door every year actually costs them. The number gets their attention. But the people behind it are the real story. And how we treat them is what we have to fix. If you've ever said "that's just the industry" out loud, and you're finally ready to see the people behind your numbers, I'd love to chat.
posts.hook: In the past year, I've spoken with distribution and manufacturing companies with 50% turnover.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: We live in New Hampshire, but we spend a lot of time in the summer visiting Vermont, just an hour away from our home. For his birthday last month I bought Dave a membership to the Vermont 251 Club. It’s not a place, really, it’s for people who love Vermont and want to visit all 251 cities and towns. We’ve visited many but we decided to start over. Yesterday we drove all back roads from home, seeing places we had never seen. We got to our B&B in Stowe and heard “oh! There’s a problem with your room!” Not something you love to hear hours from home. When most inns we love were fully booked. So we took a chance and headed toward Warren, Dave called a place I had always wanted to stay at, they had a room, and we ended up with this beautiful view, and a cafe across the street. So it worked out much better than our original plan, actually! Life is funny like that. And I’m thrilled I got to drink iced espresso from the Warren Store on a beautiful morning with a beautiful view. Our original plan fell apart, and we ended up with something better. A good thought to take with me into the work week!
posts.hook: We live in New Hampshire, but we spend a lot of time in the summer visiting Vermont, just an hour away from our home.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Building is not optional for me as the founder and Chief People Officer of my own company. The work with my clients is the heart of what I do, but a significant portion of my time goes into building, from my newsletter to my book to exclusive tools for my distribution and manufacturing subscribers (watch for the latest in a few days - it’s one I get asked for often!) to the future of my business, to so much more. I’m a strategist, but I’m also a builder. And if I were still in the corporate world, I would definitely be a builder. If you are leading HR or People teams, even as a department of one, building is no longer optional. The tools exist now for you to build the things you’ve always wished you had. The onboarding experience you sketched out but never had budget for. The manager toolkit living in your head. The process you know would save your team hours every week. You don’t need a developer. You don’t need a big budget. You need curiosity and a willingness to start before you feel ready. The HR leaders who will matter most over the next few years aren’t the ones waiting for someone to hand them a solution. They’re the ones rolling up their sleeves and making the thing. So build. Start small. Start messy. Start with the problem that’s been nagging at you the longest. Building isn’t optional for me. And it’s not optional for you either.
posts.hook: Building is not optional for me as the founder and Chief People Officer of my own company.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Almost every time I stepped on stage this year, my keynotes touched on work redesign in some way. Next week, I'm thrilled to join Hacking HR again, this time moderating a panel on "Work Redesign is Where the Real Value Lives" for the AI Experience Summit. A topic I love, and that's closely tied to the work I do on stage and wtih clients. This event explores how organizations are navigating the real impact of AI across leadership, strategy, workforce readiness, trust, governance, and ethics. Across the event, you'll be able to attend panels on on topics such as AI leadership, alignment, scaling from pilots to platforms, agentic AI in practice, measuring real impact, managing risk and regulation, and ensuring human judgment, inclusion, and psychological safety remain central as AI becomes embedded across work. Register here: https://lnkd.in/ejUyh-nr This is a fully FREE and VIRTUAL event! And it offers a FREE diploma of completion for attendees!
posts.hook: Almost every time I stepped on stage this year, my keynotes touched on work redesign in some way.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: A week later, and I cannot stop thinking about the magic of DisruptHR - Manchester, NH. I had a very long road bringing DisruptHR back to the stage in my home state, navigating grief and loss and a pandemic and leaving the corporate world and a lot more. But it finally happened! When I bought the license a couple years ago, I figured we'd hold a small event with a few speakers. Turns out, the right partner changes everything, and a large, 200 person event later, complete with a cool venue and espresso and Deadproof pizza, we're back and I am so energized for the next one. Last week I thanked Ryan and Alex, this week I want to thank our incredible speakers. When you have a lineup with 17 speakers each giving five minute talks, you hope that you have a few standout talks. But that's not what happened on June 3. Every single one was incredible. From Angela kicking it off with "when your CEO is an @$$hole" to Dirk closing with "Half a Sacred Life", and with everyone in between, we talked about the things we don't talk about enough in HR. And every speaker gave everything they had, sharing personal stories openly and vulnerably, and giving the audience real, practical takeaways. All in five minutes each. There aren't enough words to thank the speakers. So I'll just say thank you from the bottom of my heart. You are all amazing. Angela Cheng-Cimini 鄭婷婷 Pasha Marlowe, MFT Michelle Strasburger Keisha O'Marde-Jack, PHR, SHRM-CP Rebecca Conners Bruce Nichols Hema Crockett Laura Tomaino Jillian Granucci Anderson Alexis Davis, PHR, sHRBP Kelly Casey Risa Taylor Caroline Fleming, sHRBP, SHRM-CP, MBA Fleming Janelle Gorman Brooke Neva Sullivan Shelly Masson Dirk Beveridge And if you were there, or missed it and wanted to be there, watch our page for the videos. They will be released in the coming weeks! And if you want an incredible recap of the event and of each talk, check out what Leah Stackhouse wrote yesterday!
posts.hook: A week later, and I cannot stop thinking about the magic of DisruptHR - Manchester, NH.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: I've learned that I do my best work by having many things going on at once. And now that I'm two and a half years into my business that focuses on frontline companies, I am able to build and grow in a way I would not have expected. Here are just a few things I'm working on, in addition to the client work I love. These things are exclusively for those of you working in small and midsized businesses that make and move things: My book! Title to be announced soon, and to be published in September. VERY sector specific to the frontline, packed with my frameworks and thoughts and more. It's meant to be used, not just read. A proprietary culture diagnostic exclusively for frontline companies. I was working on this for a long time, and used variations of it before I got it to the point it's at today. It's incredibly impactful and extremely detailed. I kind of fought stand alone projects for awhile, but not anymore. I've loved visiting the companies who engaged with me to do this! New keynotes! While all of my keynotes are tailored for specific audiences, I've been building a lot behind the scenes, weaving in stories and data and things that can only come from someone who has worked in frontline companies for decades. And an event designed just for HR leaders in small and midsized frontline companies. This is in the very early stages, but I'm hoping to run the first small event by the end of 2026. This will represent everything I wish I had early in my career. A website refresh. Clearly focused on the work I'm doing now, for companies in distribution, manufacturing, and beyond. A webinar with Allvoices, strictly focused on frontline companies. Next month. Details shortly. Frontline companies are having a moment. It's about time!! But so much out there focuses on really large companies. I'm trying to change that. Much more soon!
posts.hook: I've learned that I do my best work by having many things going on at once.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: I've spent 30 years looking for the room built for small HR teams and departments of one in the businesses that make and move things. It doesn't exist. You know what I do when I can't find something...... I build it. More soon!
posts.hook: I've spent 30 years looking for the room built for small HR teams and departments of one in the businesses that make and move things.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Last week was so packed with excitement and emotion and exhaustion, I’m still cocooning and recovering! From the one year anniversary of my Dad’s death, I went straight into the incredible high of DisruptHR, which was everything I hoped it would be and so much more. Then Boston the following day for a Transform dinner. And through it all, getting to spend a lot of family time, and time with Alex. Saturday was a quiet day in town and at home, with coffee and scones with Dave and Jackson at Table. Sometimes I try to look at things in our hometown through the eyes of a tourist. If we stumbled on this place through our travels through Vermont, what would we think? Table is one of these adorable tucked away places, with great food, that we’d be in awe of.and it’s right in the heart of downtown Concord. I’m grateful for this past week, the friends who spoke and supported Disrupt. My family who was there to support me. And that we have so many quiet little places in the middle of our city, where I can get away without actually going away. Which is kind of nice sometimes!
posts.hook: Last week was so packed with excitement and emotion and exhaustion, I’m still cocooning and recovering!
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Meet my friend Melissa Fisk, PHR, SHRM-CP. Chief People Officer, DisruptHR - Manchester, NH committee member, and so much more. Melissa is the reason I’m a keynote speaker! Ten years ago a conversation with her led me to saying yes to getting on a stage for the very first time. That led to many other local opportunities. Which gave me the confidence to apply to a big stage in Vegas. Which led to everything else. I had not seen her in person for a very long time. But we got to spend a few minutes together at Disrupt this week. One conversation. One nudge. One “you should do this.” That can change our careers. That can change us. Melissa did that for me. And I’m not sure she even knows the full ripple effect of that one conversation a decade ago. (Though she does now!) Who saw something in you, opened a door, or gave you the push you needed? Tag them below!
posts.hook: Meet my friend Melissa Fisk, PHR, SHRM-CP.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: Our last DisruptHR - Manchester, NH was in 2019. I had incredibly high expectations for the first event in seven years. That doesn’t always work out. But yesterday, it did in the most beautiful way. Every expectation I had was exceeded, from the venue to the food and coffee (hey Espresso Dave and Deadproof!)to the speakers. The SPEAKERS. WERE. UNBELIEVABLE! More posts thanking all of you soon but for now, two people to thank. Alex Seiler for being my co-emcee, and for having the best walk up song! Thanks for flying out to do this, for hanging with my family, and for just being you. And Ryan Rourke. The most amazing co-host. When I said all I wanted to do was pick the speakers, I didn’t know that Ryan and OneDigital would handle everything. But they did! Everything! Having the license to this chapter is so personal and meaningful to me. Bringing something really different to my home state, something I wish I had years ago, I’m so grateful to get to do. And I could not have had a better co-host. Thank you Alex. And thank you Ryan!
posts.hook: Our last DisruptHR - Manchester, NH was in 2019.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: With all due respect to Miss Congeniality,the perfect date is actually June 3rd #iykyk The one thing I couldn’t control? The weather…. and it’s perfect. We’ve got a food truck, beverage truck, and live music…. out on the patio. Ah, that feels good to say! DisruptHR - Manchester, NH is today!!!! Come see what myself and Tracie Sponenberg have in store for you. 17 dazzling speakers, and we are technically SOLD OUT, But if you wanted to swing by last minute or bring a colleague along, we can make it happen. The event planner in me always builds in a buffer:) Looking forward to seeing you all today. Lets set the standard for what a DisruptHR event looks like! OneDigital #OneDigitalEast
posts.hook: With all due respect to Miss Congeniality,the perfect date is actually June 3rd #iykyk The one thing I couldn’t control?
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg

posts.text: One year ago today, we lost my dad. Grief is weird and awful and also a very strange gift that I never wanted. When I lost my mom, it was horrible but expected. I was grieving within a community of the workplace, and large communities formed at the beginning of 2020. That, and navigating pandemic response for my company, I thought, saved me. I threw myself into work, taking no time off. It turns out all that just delayed the inevitable burnout, which showed up a year and a half later. When I lost my dad, it was completely unexpected. I was grieving as a solopreneur. No teammates, no workplace. But I had communities of support that helped a lot. And I had incredibly understanding clients. I took about two weeks off to process and heal, and that made an huge difference. I had learned how to grieve in a way that worked for me. I learned what I needed, and how to lean on others. I'm not always great at regular communication with my friends or communities. But I do show up. And they did for me, in many ways this past year. So many offers to step in to help with clients. Kind gifts of food and coffee (the ones who know me well!) The friends who showed up at my dad's service, outside on one of the hottest days of the year. That means more than I could ever possibly express. I miss my dad every day. I loved that he finally understood the work I did - which is very varied and not always easy to explain! I love hearing from his clients that he shared so much about me, my family and my brother and his family, that they felt like they knew us. I loved his unique relationship with each of our kids, who he always loved exactly as they were. I loved that he grew so fond of selfies that he would ask me to take them and share them. (And I'm grateful I have so many now, like the one below!) I miss the happy birthday calls, the Happy Easter texts, the random calls, "Hey Trace, you want me to pick up some corn at McQuesten's?" whenever he golfed near the farm (which was a lot!) I miss the constant presence he was in our lives. He always showed up, he was always there, and you never had any doubt that you were loved. I got to love him for so long. And out of grief and loss, I got to know some of his friends. I get to have his very best friend in my life now, who supports me and watches out for me like my dad would have wanted him to. I get to learn stories about him, and share the funniest stories about him with our kids. It's not the same. It won't ever be the same. But it is something. Having loved my dad (and my mom), and being loved by them, for so long was a true gift that many people don't get to have. My dad always showed up. No question, no hesitation, no doubt that you were loved. And the best way I know to honor him is to try to live up to it, with my family, my friends, my clients, my communities. Show up. Be there. Let the people in your life know they are loved. He made it look easy. I'm still working on it!
posts.hook: One year ago today, we lost my dad.
posts.name: Tracie Sponenberg
